text: “But think of him now!” she cried with enthusiasm. “Think what his income allows him. His early privations are repaid a thousand times over.”

An’ noo we’re pent in dens an’ hollows,
And chased, as was brave William Wallace,
Wi’ constables—thae blackguard fallows,
An’ sodgers baith;
But Gude preserve us frae the gallows,
That shamefu’ death!

Structured knowledge graphs encode rich semantics through entities and relations (Hayashi et al., 2019). Many earlier studies have concentrated on task-oriented dialogue systems built on domain-specific knowledge bases (Zhu et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2016). To produce responses from a large-scale knowledge base, Zhou et al. (2018a) and Liu et al. (2018) employ graph attention and knowledge diffusion to choose knowledge semantics for utterance understanding and response generation. Moon et al. (2019) addresses the entity selection task and leverages positive entities that occur in the gold response. In contrast to prior work, Concept-Flow explicitly models the conversation flow with a commonsense knowledge graph and proposes a novel attention mechanism over all concepts to steer the conversation flow in latent concept space.

‘For, with strong speech I tore the veil that hid
Nature, and Truth, and Liberty, and Love,—
As one who from some mountain’s pyramid
Points to the unrisen sun!—the shades approve
His truth, and flee from every stream and grove.
Thus, gentle thoughts did many a bosom fill,—
Wisdom, the mail of tried affections wove
For many a heart, and tameless scorn of ill,
Thrice steeped in molten steel the unconquerable will.
“Maybe he’s made of different stuff from what it takes to wear them,” said Gabriel grimly. “Well, that’s enough of this. Go on, Cainy—quick.”

Explicit Denoising The model should be able to remove noise from the encodings before decoding the text. While previous methods (Vincent et al., 2008; Freitag and Roy, 2018) implicitly assign the denoising task to the encoder, we propose an explicit denoising component (see module (b) in Figure 2 ). Specifically, we create a correction vector c ... j :

We sample a review as a candidate summary and generate noisy versions thereof, using two functions: (a) segment noising adds noise at the token and chunk level, and (b) document noising adds noise at the text level. The noise functions are illustrated in Figure 1 . Figure 1: Synthetic dataset creation. Given a sampled candidate summary, we add noise using two methods: (a) segment noising performs token-and chunk-level alterations, and (b) document noising replaces the text with a semantically similar review.

Model architecture: As the name suggests, RoBERTa-DDI relies on pre-trained RoBERTa representations (Liu et al., 2019) to encode the input sequences. For further details on BERT and the transformer architecture, we refer readers to Liu et al. 2019, Devlin et al. (2018), and Vaswani et al. (2017). In RoBERTa-DDI, we attach a dropout layer followed by a single feedforward output layer with a softmax nonlinearity. This layer takes as input the [CLS] token representation from the top transformer layer and produces probabilities over the labels {0, 1}, where 1 denotes an interaction.

And is this death?—The pyre hath vanished clean,  
The Pestilence, the Tyrant, and the throng;  
The flames fall hush’d—by slow degrees is seen  
The music of a breath-detaining song,  
Which, like the kiss of love when life is young,  
Steeps the faint eyes in darkness sweet and deep;  
With ever-shifting notes it glides along,  
Till on my passive soul there seem’d to creep  
A melody, like waves on wrinkled sands that leap.

“But there is still time,” said Brissenden. “Who knows but that you may yet prove the humble means of saving him. Why didn’t you let me swat him just once? I should have liked to have had a hand in it.”
“She most of all,” said Ernest, “requires consolation; she accused herself of having caused the death of my brother, and that made her very wretched. But since the murderer has been discovered—”

The motivation behind the experiments reported in Table 1 is as follows: we extracted word embeddings from pre-trained BERT and XLNet models and trained the baseline models using those embeddings. Since BERT and XLNet are often perceived to provide better word embeddings than Glove, it is not fair to compare MAG-BERT/MAG-XLNet with previous models trained with Glove embeddings. Therefore, we retrain previous works us- Table 1 : Sentiment prediction results on CMU-MOSI dataset. Best results are highlighted in bold. MAG-BERT and MAG-XLNet achieve superior performance than the baselines and their language-only finetuned counterpart. BA denotes binary accuracy (higher is better, same for F1), MAE denotes Mean-absolute Error (lower is better), and Corr is Pearson Correlation (higher is better). For BA and F1, we report two numbers: the number on the left side of "/" is measures calculated based on (Zadeh et al., 2018c) and the right side is measures calculated based on (Tsai et al., 2019) . Human performance for CMU-MOSI is reported as (Zadeh et al., 2018a) . "E" denotes application of MAG immediately after embedding layer of the XLNet and "A" denotes applying MAG after the embedding layer and all the subsequent Encoding layers. ⊕ and ⊙ denote input-level addition and concatenation of all modalities respectively. MAG applied at initial layers performs better overall.

Optimization tools, such as SVM-struct (Joachims, 2008), are concerned with parameter estimation. Model libraries, such as CRFSuite (Okazaki, 2007), CRF++ (Kudo, 2005), and NCRF++ (Yang and Zhang, 2018), provide inference for a fixed collection of widely used models, typically linear-chain CRFs. General-purpose inference libraries, such as PyStruct (Müller and Behnke, 2014) or TurboParser (Martins et al., 2010), rely on external solvers for inference, chiefly MAP inference, including integer linear programming solvers and ADMM. Probabilistic programming languages, for instance languages integrated with deep learning such as Pyro (Bingham et al., 2019), enable specification and inference over some discrete domains. More ambitiously, inference libraries such as Dyna (Eisner et al., 2004) support declarative descriptions of dynamic programming algorithms to enable inference for generic algorithms. Torch-Struct adopts a different strategy, integrating a library of optimized structured distributions into a vectorized deep learning framework. We start by motivating this design with a case study.

I yield nothing as duties,
What others yield as duties I yield as living impulses,
(Shall I make the heart’s action a duty?)

And I have dreamed that the aim and heart of the life we know,  
the passing,  
is to shape and determine the self of the life unseen, the lasting.

Months have slipped by in lingering circles,
Each night the specter makes its way,
With trembling tread it stirs the ground,
With trembling tread it wanders near me still—

SUMMA (Liepins et al., 2017) is another comparable system that integrates a range of NLP components and supports multiple media types and languages, enabling the simultaneous monitoring of several media broadcasts. SUMMA employs the online clustering algorithm of Aggarwal and Yu (2006) and the extractive summarization algorithm of Almeida and Martins (2013). Unlike NSTM, SUMMA is designed for settings involving continuous multimedia and multilingual data streams, and it generates substantially longer summaries.

“You could not discover the place to which these people had gone,” said Monks, “but where friendship breaks down, hatred will often make a passage. My mother found it, after a year of crafty searching—ay, and found the child.”

For RL-based text generation, the majority of existing studies adopt a model-free framework, which has been criticized for its high variance and low sample efficiency (Sutton and Barto, 1998). By contrast, although model-based RL methods do not exhibit these drawbacks, they are generally difficult to train in complex environments. Moreover, the capacity of the environment model typically constrains the learned policy. Recent advances in model-based RL (Gu et al., 2016; Kurutach et al., 2018; Nagabandi et al., 2017) integrate the strengths of both paradigms and have yielded better performance by learning a model-free policy with the aid of an environment model. In addition, model-based RL has recently been applied to tasks with extremely sparse rewards through curiosity-driven methods (Pathak et al., 2017).

Beneath the teachings of things, spirits, Nature, governments,  
possessions, I swear I discern other teachings,  
Beneath it all, to me, is myself; to you, yourself, (the same  
dull, age-old song.)

Model 2: Phone End-to-End. Our final model uses phone-factored embeddings, where trainable embeddings for phone features are concatenated to typical speech feature vector input. Because phone durations are variable and typically span more than one filterbank feature (or frame), adjacent filterbank features may have the predicted phone label; in the example shown in Figure 1, /R/ spans three frames or filterbank features. We note that this method maintains the same source sequence length as the original speech feature sequence. This method associates similar feature vectors at the corpus level, because all filterbank features with the same phone alignment (e.g. /OH/) will have the same trainable phone embedding concatenated. In MT and NER, concatenating trainable embeddings for linguistic features to words, such as morphemes and phones, has improved models' ability to generalize (Sennrich and Haddow, 2016; Chaudhary et al., 2018) . While these works appended finer-grained information to associate words with similar lower-level structure, we use phone embeddings to associate higher-level structure to similar but unique speech feature vectors globally across a corpus.

The rationale for the experiments reported in Table 1 is as follows: we extracted word embeddings from pre-trained BERT and XLNet models and used those embeddings to train the baseline models. Because BERT and XLNet are often regarded as yielding superior word embeddings to GloVe, it would be unfair to compare MAG-BERT/MAG-XLNet with prior models trained on GloVe embeddings. We therefore retrain earlier work using these embeddings. Table 1 presents sentiment prediction results on the CMU-MOSI dataset, with the best results highlighted in bold. MAG-BERT and MAG-XLNet achieve better performance than the baselines and their language-only fine-tuned counterpart. BA denotes binary accuracy (higher is better, as is F1), MAE denotes mean absolute error (lower is better), and Corr denotes Pearson correlation (higher is better). For BA and F1, we report two values: the number to the left of “/” is computed following Zadeh et al. (2018c), and the number to the right is computed following Tsai et al. (2019). Human performance on CMU-MOSI is reported in Zadeh et al. (2018a). Table 2 reports results for variations of the XLNet model, including MAG applied at different layers of XLNet, as well as input-level concatenation and addition of all modalities. “E” denotes applying MAG immediately after the embedding layer of XLNet, whereas “A” denotes applying MAG after the embedding layer and all subsequent encoding layers. ⊕ and ⊙ denote input-level addition and concatenation of all modalities, respectively. Overall, applying MAG at the initial layers performs better.

The quivering vapours of the hazy noontide,  
Which like a sea o’er the warm earth do glide,  
Wherein every sound, and odour, and beam,  
Move, as reeds in one single stream;

Reckoning ahead O soul, when thou, the time achiev’d,
The seas all cross’d, weather’d the capes, the voyage done,
Surrounded, copest, frontest God, yieldest, the aim attain’d,
As fill’d with friendship, love complete, the Elder Brother found,
The Younger melts in fondness in his arms.

So sailors say, on yesterday,
Just as the dusk was brown,
One little boat gave up its strife,
And gurgled down and down.

‘’Twas like an eye which seemed to smile on me.
I watched, till by the sun made pale, it sank
Under the billows of the heaving sea;
But from its beams deep love my spirit drank,
And to my brain the boundless world now shrank
Into one thought—one image—yes, for ever!
Even like the dayspring, poured on vapours dank,
The beams of that one Star did shoot and quiver
Through my benighted mind—and were extinguished never.

As one by one the stately players vanish,
From that vast drama on history’s endless stage,
That glaring, partisan scene of war and peace—of old and new in conflict,
Wrought through rage, through fear, through midnight dread, and many a weary pause;
All gone—and since, in numberless graves receding, softening,
Conqueror’s and vanquish’d—Lincoln’s and Lee’s—now thou with them,
Man of the mighty days—and match’d to the days!
Thou from the prairies!—tangled and many-vein’d and hard hath been thy lot,
To wonder has it been performed!

O Liberty! O companion fit for me!  
Here, too, the blaze, the grape-shot, and the axe, held back in readiness, to bring  
them forth in time of need,  
Here, too, though long suppressed, can never be destroyed,  
Here, too, at last might rise, murderous and ecstatic,  
Here, too, demanding all the debt of vengeance paid.

We note that semi-structured premises compelled annotators to invoke world knowledge and common sense (KCS); 48.75% of instances in the Dev set require KCS. By comparison, in the MultiNLI corpus, KCS is required for 25.72% of examples. We suspect that this is because information about the entities and their types is not explicitly provided in tables and must therefore be inferred. In making these inferences, our annotators drew on their knowledge of the world, including facts about weather, seasons, and widely recognized social and cultural norms and facts. One illustration of such common sense is the hypothesis that “X was born in summer” for a person whose date of birth is in May in New York. We expect that the INFOTABS data can serve as a foundation for investigating common-sense reasoning, alongside other recent work such as that of Talmor et al. (2019),Neutral hypotheses are more likely to be subjective or out-of-table because nearly anything subjective, or anything not mentioned in the table, is a neutral statement. Even so, we found that in all evaluations in Appendix E, except those involving the adversarial α 2 test set, our models found neutrals almost as difficult as the other two labels, with only an ≈ 3% gap between the F-scores of the neutral label and the next best label.

Dearest comrades, all is over and long gone,
But love is not over--and what love, O comrades!
Perfume from battle-fields rising, up from the foetor arising.

Human Evaluation We also conducted two judgment elicitation studies using the Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT) crowdsourcing platform. The first study assessed the quality of the summaries using Best-Worst Scaling (BWS; Louviere et al., 2015) , a less labor-intensive alternative to paired comparisons that has been shown to produce more reliable results than rating scales (Kiritchenko and Mohammad, 2017) . Specifically, participants were shown the movie/business name, some basic background information, and a gold-standard summary. They were also presented with three system summaries, produced by SENTINEURON (best extractive model), MEANSUM (most related unsupervised model), and DENOISESUM.

Baselines. We compare ExpBERT against several baselines that train a classifier over the same input representation u(x). NoExp trains a classifier only on u(x). The other baselines augment u(x) with variants of the explanation representation v(x). BERT+SemParser uses the semantic parser from Hancock et al. (2018) to convert explanations into executable logical forms. The resulting denotations over the input x (a single bit for each explanation) are used as the explanation representation, i.e., v(x) ∈ {0, 1} n . We use two different sets of explanations for this baseline: our natural language explanations (LangExp) and the low-level explanations from Hancock et al. (2018) that are more suitable for the semantic parser (ProgExp). BERT+Patterns converts explanations into a collection of unigram, bigram, and trigram patterns and creates a binary feature for each pattern based on whether it is contained in s or not. This gives v(x) ∈ {0, 1} n , where n is the number of patterns. Finally, we compare ExpBERT against a

Then my realities;
What else is so real as mine?
Libertad and the divine average, freedom to every slave on the face
of the earth,
The rapt promises and lumine of seers, the spiritual world, these
centuries-lasting songs,
And our visions, the visions of poets, the most solid announcements
of any.

Hark to me, ye reverend core,
As counsel for poor mortals
Who oft do pass through Wisdom’s door
For Folly’s foolish portals:
I, for their heedless, careless sake,
Would here propose defences—
Their droll misdeeds, their dark mistakes,
Their failings and mischance.

Who made the heart, ’tis He alone  
Can surely search and prove us;  
He knows each chord, its varied tone,  
Each spring, its several bias:  
Then at the balance let us cease,  
Nor seek to set it right;  
What’s done we may in part surmise,  
But know not what withstood it.

Nature never did betray
The heart that loved her; 'tis her privilege,
Through all the years of this our life, to lead
From joy to joy: for she can so inform
The mind that is within us, so impress
With quietness and beauty, and so feed
With lofty thoughts, that neither evil tongues,
Rash judgments, nor the sneers of selfish men,
Nor greetings where no kindness is, nor all
The dreary intercourse of daily life,
Shall e'er prevail against us, or disturb
Our cheerful faith, that all which we behold
Is full of blessings.

The two old, simple problems ever intertwined,
Close home, elusive, present, baffled, grappled.
By each successive age insoluble, pass’d on,
To ours to-day--and we pass on the same.

By the mossy brink,
With me the Prince shall sit and think;
Shall muse in visioned Regency,
Rapt in bright dreams of dawning Royalty.

Poor Man, the fly, doth oft hum past thy side,  
And oft, by chance, as he draweth thee nigh,  
Thy cursed old elbow itcheth up for joy  
And hellish pleasure!  
Already in thy fancy’s eye,  
Thy sicker treasure.

‘Time passed—I know not whether months or years;
For day, nor night, nor turn of seasons made
Its mark, but thoughts and tears that nothing cheers:
And I grew, in the end, as one half-shade,
A smoke, a cloud the winds have worn and frayed,
Till it was thin as air; until, one eve,
A Nautilus upon the fountain strayed,
Unfurling his blue sail where Heaven’s breath did not leave,
Among the waves and whirling eddies driven.

Space is roofless and bare,
And in the midst a cloudy shrine,
Dark amid thrones of light.
In the blue glow of hyaline
Golden worlds revolve and shine.
In ... flight
From every point of the Infinite,
Like a thousand dawns on a single night
The splendours rise and spread;
And through thunder and darkness dread
Light and music are radiated,
And in their pavilioned chariots led
By living wings high overhead
The giant Powers move,
Gloomy or bright as the thrones they fill.

Throb! throb, ye drums!--blare! bugles, blare!
Above the traffic of cities--above the clamor of wheels in the streets;
Are couches prepared for sleepers by night in the houses? no sleepers
may lie in those couches,
No trader’s bargains by day--no brokers or speculators--should
they endure?
Would the talkers be talking? would the singer essay to sing?
Would the lawyer rise in the court to plead his cause before the judge?
Then clatter swifter, weightier drums--ye bugles, wilder, blow.

The men cast out in party-matches,
Then sowther a’ in deep debauches.
Ae night they’re mad wi’ drink an’ whoring,
Niest day their life is past enduring.

not there alone,
Nor for his mate, nor for himself alone, nor for all the echoes thrown back,
But something delicate, secret, beyond and away,
A charge conveyed and hidden gift for those yet to be born.

Farewell to the straths and green vallies below;
Farewell to the forests and wild-hanging woods,
Farewell to the torrents and loud-pouring floods.
My heart’s in the Highlands, &c.

Decoding Strategies Thus far, we have only evaluated perplexity, whereas our actual objective is generation, which necessitates decoding. We examine several standard approaches: greedy decoding; beam search, with beam size and minimum and maximum output length as hyperparameters; beam search with blocking, which prevents repeated n-grams, where we use n = 3 (Paulus et al., 2018); and nucleus sampling, parameterized by p (Holtzman et al., 2019). The impact of these choices is reported in Table 6 for ConvAI2 and Wizard of Wikipedia (WoW).

I carry them, men and women, I carry them with me wherever I go,
I swear it is impossible for me to get rid of them,

With me the Prince shall sit and think;
Shall muse in visioned Regency,
Enrapt in bright dreams of dawning Royalty.

A related but distinct research area, readability, examines the reading difficulty of a text body. This area has explored readability effects across a wide range of contexts, including online versus paper media (Kurniawan and Zaphiris, 2001), color and contrast (Legge et al., 1990), and writing style (Bostian, 1983). The most widely known readability metric for English, Flesch-Kincaid (Kincaid et al., 1975), determines readability using counts of syllables and words. Other researchers have sought to refine this straightforward metric, demonstrating successful reading-level classification with unigram language models (Si and Callan, 2001) or SVM models constructed from these basic textual features (Pitler and Nenkova, 2008). Since earlier metrics appear to be adequate, recent work has concentrated on evaluating and comparing a variety of metrics across different domains (Sugawara et al., 2017; Redmiles et al., 2019). We draw on this readability literature to inform our feature selection, as readability seems to be intrinsically tied to reading time. Accordingly, we use the py-readability-metrics package to incorporate seven state-of-the-art metrics, which we add to our data for the modeling task (Section 4, Appendix B).

Of skies so beauteous after a storm, and at night the moon so

Shining sweetly, shining down, where we dig the trenches and
gather the heaps,
I dream, I dream, I dream.

Hyperparameters We use the uncased BERT-Base (Devlin et al., 2019) to calculate contextual embeddings for all models. We use ADAM (Kingma and Ba, 2015) to train the models with batch size 4 and a learning rate of 1e-5. For the CRF framework, we learn the scaling parameter λ during training, which is important to get stable results. For L-TapNet, we set α as 0.5 and β as 0.7. We fine-tune BERT with Gradual Unfreezing trick (Howard and Ruder, 2018) . Of that silver orb,  
Whose fierce beam thins  
In the clear white dawn  
Until we can scarcely see it—we only feel it there.

If I should not be living
When the robins come,
Leave the one with scarlet cravat
A memorial crumb.

Musicians wrestle everywhere:
All day, among the crowded air,
I hear the silver strife;
And -- waking long before the dawn --
Such transport breaks upon the town
I think it that "new life!"
